name: Energy & commodities
description: Crude, natural gas, fuels, electricity, and production/stocks for energy producers, refiners, and utilities.

series.note: The benchmark U.S. crude price that drives upstream revenue, drilling economics, and the WTI-Brent spread; the single most important energy price indicator.
industries: E&P / Upstream (XOM, CVX, COP, EOG, OXY, DVN, FANG)

series.note: The global seaborne crude benchmark; the WTI-Brent differential signals U.S. export economics and refiner feedstock advantages.
The U.S. natural gas benchmark that drives gas producer cash flow, LNG export margins, and utility fuel costs.
Retail gasoline price at the pump is a direct consumer demand and discretionary-spending signal and a key refiner downstream margin component.
Diesel is the lifeblood fuel of freight, rail, and agriculture, making it a leading cost input and industrial-demand barometer.
Weekly WTI smooths daily noise for cleaner trend analysis of crude price cycles affecting producer hedging and capex decisions.
Monthly Henry Hub gas price provides a smoothed basis for tracking gas-fired utility fuel costs and producer realized prices.
Utility output tracks electricity and gas generation volumes, a direct read on regulated and merchant power demand.
Retail electricity price is a regulated-utility revenue and inflation signal, and a cost input for energy-intensive manufacturers.
Wholesale power-distribution PPI captures cost-side pricing pressure across the regulated electric value chain.
Crude petroleum PPI tracks wholesale feedstock cost inflation flowing into refiners, chemicals, and the broader goods PPI.
The broad energy-PPI bucket is the canonical gauge of fuel and power cost inflation feeding into every energy-intensive sector's margins.
Refinery output PPI captures product-price realizations, the key to refining crack spreads and downstream margin analysis.
Coal PPI tracks thermal and met-coal price trends affecting coal producers and coal-fired generation fuel costs.
Copper is the bellwether industrial metal ('Dr. Copper') signaling global growth, electrification demand, and base-metal miner economics.
European TTF-linked gas price drives U.S. LNG export arbitrage and global gas spreads versus Henry Hub.
A broad all-commodity index gives the top-down inflation and global-demand backdrop framing energy and materials cycles.
The dollar is inversely correlated with dollar-priced commodities; a stronger USD pressures crude, metals, and energy-producer realizations.
